Findings
Visual Quality
▶ ✘ Colour Contrast 6 findings ✘ FAIL
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Navigation links: "What we do", "Who we are", "How we partner", "Case studies", "Insights", "Get in touch"
Detail
The navigation links in the header (white text on a bright pink background) appear to have insufficient contrast. While difficult to confirm exact ratio without color values, white on this saturated pink is highly suspect for AA compliance.
Fix
Verify the contrast ratio for white text against the magenta background. If it falls below 4.5:1, use a slightly darker foreground color or a less saturated background color.
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Logo text "RUSH"
Detail
The red/pink portion of the RUSH logo against the white background near the top left appears to have low contrast, failing 4.5:1 for normal text.
Fix
Ensure the red/pink used in the logo portion meets a 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the white background.
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Video error message text: "We couldn't verify the security of your connection." and "Access to this content has been restricted. Contact your internet service provider for help."
Detail
The white text inside the black video player area has sufficient contrast (white text on black background is 21:1). However, the status is 'fail' because the subsequent elements below the main banner might be affected.
Fix
This section passes contrast (white on black). No suggestion needed here unless other text elements fail.
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Text in the main content area (Section titles and descriptions)
Detail
All text blocks below the main banner (e.g., 'Digital Strategy & Innovation', and its description) are white text against the same bright pink background. This is highly likely to fail WCAG AA contrast requirements (4.5:1) for normal text, similar to the navigation links.
Fix
Use a darker text color (or slightly off-white) for these body/heading texts against the bright pink background, or change the background color.
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Footer links and text ("Follow us", "Find us", address, footer navigation links)
Detail
The text in the footer (white/light grey text on a very dark grey/black background) appears insufficient. Specifically, the smaller text like the address and footer navigation links ('Terms of Use', 'Site Accessibility', etc.) seems low contrast against the dark background.
Fix
Increase the contrast ratio of the lighter grey text in the footer against the dark background to meet at least 4.5:1 for regular text and 3:1 for large text/UI components (if applicable).
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Newsletter form inputs and buttons
Detail
The 'Enter your email' input field shows light text or placeholder text against a dark background, and the 'Subscribe' button has light text against a dark background. The contrast seems low for the text elements within these components.
Fix
Ensure the text inside the email input field and the text label on the 'Subscribe' button meet the 4.5:1 contrast ratio against their respective backgrounds (the input field border/background and the button background).
▶ ⚠ Colour Blindness Simulation 2 findings ⚠ WARN
⚠ WARN WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
White text against bright pink background (Header navigation, main content text)
Detail
Under Deuteranopia and Protanopia simulations (red/green weakness), the saturation and perceived brightness of the pink background might shift, potentially lowering the contrast against the white foreground text, although white on high saturation color often maintains visibility unless the color hue is close to white's perceived brightness. The primary failure here is the contrast itself (see colour-contrast check).
Fix
Address the underlying contrast failures. Ensure sufficient luminance contrast, which is less affected by hue shifts associated with red-green color blindness.
⚠ WARN WCAG AA · 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)
Element
Footer elements (Light grey text on black)
Detail
Under Tritanopia simulation (blue/yellow weakness), the perception of the dark grey/black background might change slightly, potentially affecting the contrast of the light grey text, though this area is generally safe if luminance contrast is high enough.
Fix
Verify the luminance contrast, especially for smaller footer text, which is the key metric for color blindness accessibility in low-light scenarios.
▶ ℹ Rendered Text Size 2 findings ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG AA · 1.4.4 Resize text
Element
Body text blocks in the main content section
Detail
The body text descriptions under the service headings appear relatively small. Without knowing the viewport size or base font size, it is impossible to confirm if they render below 12px (or 16px equivalent based on standard 100% zoom). They look smaller than ideal for body copy.
Fix
Ensure base font size allows body text to render at a minimum of 16px equivalent, and if smaller, ensure text content is large enough (at least 12px for non-essential text).
ℹ INFO WCAG AA · 1.4.4 Resize text
Element
Footer text (Address, legal links)
Detail
The text in the footer section, including the address and copyright information, appears significantly smaller than the main body text and navigation links.
Fix
Increase the font size for footer legal/utility links and address details to be more readable, ideally at least 12px rendered height or matching the base body size if possible.
▶ ⚠ Visual Density & Crowding 2 findings ⚠ WARN
⚠ WARN WCAG info · 1.1 Text Spacing
Element
Text blocks in the main service description area
Detail
The vertical spacing between paragraphs within the same service description (e.g., under 'Digital Strategy & Innovation') appears slightly cramped, although the overall layout uses large blocks of negative space.
Fix
Increase line height (leading) and paragraph spacing for blocks of descriptive text to improve readability and reduce visual density.
⚠ WARN WCAG info · 1.4 Spacing
Element
Footer content columns
Detail
The four columns in the footer ('Follow us', 'Find us', 'Contact us', 'Newsletter') are visually distinct but the content within 'Find us' (address block) is quite dense compared to the other, more sparse sections.
Fix
Slightly increase vertical spacing within the address block in the 'Find us' section to match the generally spacious layout elsewhere on the page.
▶ ✘ Focus Indicator Visibility 1 finding ✘ FAIL
✘ FAIL WCAG AA · 2.4.7 Focus Visible
Element
All interactive elements (Header navigation, footer links, form inputs)
Detail
No visible focus indicators are present for any interactive elements when examining the static screenshot. It is assumed that when navigated via keyboard, focus rings will be absent or insufficient.
Fix
Implement a highly visible focus indicator (e.g., a thick outline or a distinct color change) for all interactive elements when they receive keyboard focus.
▶ ✔ Animation & Flashing 1 finding ✔ PASS
✔ PASS WCAG A · 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold
Element
Entire page content
Detail
There is no visible flashing, strobing, or rapid animation content that would trigger photosensitive conditions.
Fix
None required.

⚠ WARN WCAG AA · 2.5.5 Target size (Enhanced)
Element
Header navigation links (e.g., "What we do", "Get in touch")
Detail
The navigation links appear as text elements without visible padding or surrounding clickable areas, suggesting they might have a touch target area smaller than the recommended 44x44px.
Fix
Ensure all interactive text links in the header have an accessible target size of at least 44x44 CSS pixels.
⚠ WARN WCAG AA · 2.5.5 Target size (Enhanced)
Element
Play sound link/icon in video player area
Detail
The 'Play sound' control appears small, consisting of a waveform icon and text. It is likely smaller than 44x44px.
Fix
Ensure the interactive element for 'Play sound' has a minimum target size of 44x44px.
⚠ WARN WCAG AA · 2.5.5 Target size (Enhanced)
Element
Newsletter Subscribe button and Email Input field
Detail
The 'Subscribe' button and the 'Enter your email' input field are rectangular elements. While the input field has decent width, their height might be less than 44px, and they appear close together, potentially failing target size criteria if the input field itself is too small.
Fix
Ensure the 'Subscribe' button and the input field area meet a minimum target size of 44x44px vertically.
⚠ WARN WCAG A · 2.5.5 Target size (Minimum)
Element
Footer links ('Terms of Use', 'Site Accessibility', 'Privacy Policy', 'Get in touch' arrow)
Detail
The small text links in the footer may have touch targets smaller than the minimum 24x24px required by WCAG A if they rely only on the text size for click area.
Fix
Ensure all discrete links in the footer, including the arrow next to 'Get in touch', have an accessible target size of at least 24x24 CSS pixels.
▶ ⚠ Reading Order vs Visual Flow 2 findings ⚠ WARN
⚠ WARN WCAG A · 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence
Element
Header navigation bar
Detail
The logo 'RUSH' is visually on the far left, followed by the navigation links. If the DOM order places the navigation links before the logo or in an unexpected sequence, screen reader users might experience a confusing flow.
Fix
Ensure the DOM reading order follows the visual flow: Logo -> Navigation Links (left to right).
⚠ WARN WCAG A · 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence
Element
Main content service blocks
Detail
The services are presented in three columns visually. If the underlying HTML nests them linearly (e.g., Col 1 Row 1, Col 2 Row 1, Col 3 Row 1, then Col 1 Row 2), the reading order should follow this left-to-right, top-to-bottom flow explicitly via tab order and DOM structure.
Fix
Verify that the DOM structure presents the services in the clear reading order: Digital Strategy & Innovation -> AI Strategy & Enablement -> Digital CX & Product, followed by the next row.
▶ ✔ Text Reflow at 200% Zoom 1 finding ✔ PASS
✔ PASS WCAG A · 1.4.10 Reflow
Element
All content
Detail
The content appears to be structured primarily in vertical stacks (main hero section, services grid in the footer area). The hero text is large, but it seems designed to stack vertically ('Make better el.'). Assuming standard use of CSS techniques like flexbox/grid with responsive breakpoints handled by the underlying Webflow implementation, it is highly likely the content will reflow correctly to a single column at 320px width without requiring horizontal scrolling, based on the visual layout provided.
Fix
No immediate action is necessary based on visual inspection, as the layout appears responsive. Verify the behavior at 200% zoom using browser developer tools.
▶ ✘ Icon-Only Buttons 1 finding ✘ FAIL
✘ FAIL WCAG A · 1.1.1 Non-text Content
Element
Play sound icon/link in the video player area
Detail
There is an element labeled 'Play sound' next to a visual representation of sound waves/equalizer bars within the restricted video player area. The HTML indicates this element is an anchor tag that likely controls video/audio playback state or volume. It is visually represented as an icon/text combination, but its accessibility relies on the combination. Since the video player is embedded via an iframe (`<iframe src="https://player.vimeo.com/video/888902333?..."`), the accessibility of the internal controls is often managed by the Vimeo player itself. However, for the visible 'Play sound' text/icon overlay, it must have a proper accessible name if it acts as a button.
Fix
Ensure the link/button associated with 'Play sound' (which seems to be visually layered over the Vimeo player) has an accessible name for screen readers. If the iframe content itself is not accessible, the custom overlay must clearly convey its function, perhaps by being part of a larger accessible control group, or by ensuring the underlying Vimeo controls are accessible when focused.
▶ ℹ Custom Controls 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG A · 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value
Element
Navigation dropdown triggers
Detail
The navigation links act as custom controls to show/hide large dropdown menus (Mega Menus) using JavaScript (indicated by `data-dropdown-trigger="1"`). Although they use anchor tags (`<a>`) styled as buttons, they prevent default action and rely on custom JS. Their role is functionally a menu button. While they are visibly labeled (e.g., 'What we do'), their role as interactive toggles should ideally be communicated to assistive technology, perhaps via `role="button"` or appropriate ARIA properties if they are not functioning as standard links.
Fix
Review the ARIA attributes on elements triggering the dropdown menus (e.g., 'What we do', 'Who we are'). If these links only trigger menu expansion/collapse and don't navigate immediately, consider adding `role="button"`, `aria-expanded`, and ensuring they are keyboard focusable if they are not natively focusable links.

▶ ✔ ARIA Live Regions 1 finding ✔ PASS
✔ PASS WCAG A · 4.1.3 Status Messages
Element
Form submission messages
Detail
The form submission success/error messages in the footer newsletter area (`.w-form-done`, `.w-form-fail`) are standard Webflow dynamic messages. Webflow often handles making these visible elements announce changes via ARIA live regions implicitly or through custom JS/configuration, but without seeing the execution environment, we look for explicit ARIA attributes. Since no explicit `aria-live` is visible in the static HTML, this relies on Webflow's default handling for form validation/completion messages.
Fix
Verify that the success/error messages (`.w-form-done`, `.w-form-fail`) are correctly announced to screen readers upon submission. If they are not automatically announced, they should be wrapped in an element with `role="status"` or `aria-live="polite"`.
Cognitive Accessibility
▶ ⚠ Reading Level 1 finding ⚠ WARN
⚠ WARN WCAG AAA · 3.1.5 Reading Level
Element
Main body text content
Detail
The main text content on the homepage (e.g., 'We design and build secure AI-first digital experiences for ambitious enterprises.' and KPI descriptions) contains technical/business jargon ('AI-first', 'ambitious enterprises', 'digital product strategy', 'DevOps', 'ISO-certified cybersecurity') which suggests the reading level might be complex, potentially exceeding Grade 9. Since this is the main page content, if the estimated grade level is high, it flags for an issue.
Fix
Provide a simplified summary or plain language explanation for complex sections, or ensure the Flesch-Kincaid grade level for primary information does not exceed grade 9.
▶ ✘ Content Hierarchy 1 finding ✘ FAIL
✘ FAIL WCAG A · 1.3.1 Info and Relationships
Element
Heading structure (h1, h2, h3)
Detail
The main heading is an H1: "Make\nbetter\n. ...". While semantically correct for the page title, the structure immediately following relies heavily on dynamically loaded or styled content (`.typed`, `.blackdotoswald`) rather than standard, well-structured headings to outline major sections. Section titles like 'Impact', 'What we do', and 'Insights' appear as H2 or H3 elements, but they are dynamically faded/styled (`style="opacity: 0;"`) which might affect assistive technology parsing during initial load, and the main visual flow relies on complex dynamic elements (typing cursor, animated word lists).
Fix
Ensure that all major sections on the page have clear, nested heading structures (H1, H2, H3, etc.) that are consistently styled and present immediately upon DOM loading, rather than relying heavily on content that appears/animates later or relies on visual cues/animations for structure.
▶ ℹ Instruction Clarity 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG A · 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions
Element
Navigation Menu/Dropdowns
Detail
The primary navigation uses complex, multi-level dynamic dropdown menus triggered by clicking top-level links (e.g., 'What we do'). Instructions on how to navigate these menus are implied by the UI design (e.g., clicking a link opens a large overlay). While the JS attempts to manage mobile vs desktop views, the process of opening a submenu, seeing the options, and closing it relies heavily on visual state changes and JavaScript interactions, which can be confusing procedurally.
Fix
For interactive elements that reveal significant content (like the megamenu), ensure the instructions are explicit (e.g., 'Click to expand sections') or that the expanded state clearly indicates the current context and how to return to the main menu. The use of 'data-dropdown-trigger="1"' implies instruction/control, but it's not explicitly user-facing instruction.
▶ ⚠ Jargon Density 1 finding ⚠ WARN
⚠ WARN WCAG info · Information source/style
Element
Service descriptions and KPI captions
Detail
The page uses significant industry-specific jargon, including 'AI-first', 'digital product strategy', 'Design Systems', 'Human Centred Design', 'DevOps', 'ISO-certified cybersecurity', and 'Computer Vision' ('R/VISION'). While appropriate for a specialized audience, high density may exclude users unfamiliar with these specific technology/consulting terms.
Fix
Where possible, pair technical terms with simpler explanations or definitions, especially in areas like the KPI descriptions (e.g., instead of 'Digital Product Strategy', consider phrasing like 'Strategy for digital products').
Inclusivity & Diversity informational · does not affect WCAG score

These findings are best-practice recommendations that go beyond WCAG compliance. They cover representation, language inclusivity, cultural assumptions, and geographic bias. None of these items affect the overall pass/fail result — they are provided to help build a more welcoming experience for all users.

Visual
▶ ℹ Representation in Imagery 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG info · Inclusive Imagery
Element
Image of people clapping in a meeting setting.
Detail
The visible portion of the image depicting people clapping shows a group primarily composed of men, with limited visible representation of women or individuals of diverse ethnic backgrounds. This risks reinforcing a stereotype of technology/enterprise roles being male-dominated.
Fix
Ensure imagery used for general corporate engagement (like meetings or collaboration) features visible diversity across gender, age, and ethnicity.
▶ ℹ Symbolic Bias 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG info · Avoid Cultural Defaulting
Element
Address block in footer: "2 Marston Street, Parnell, Auckland 1052"
Detail
The physical address provided in the footer explicitly names Auckland, New Zealand. While this is fine if the primary audience is NZ-based, the overall site structure and language are professional/international. Providing only a single, specific geographic location (Auckland, NZ) as the default contact point might imply the service scope is limited regionally, potentially excluding international users or partners unless explicitly stated elsewhere.
Fix
If the company operates globally, ensure contact methods include international or digital-only options prominently, or clearly state geographic limitations if applicable. If the domain/context implies NZ focus (.co.nz), this is less critical but still worth noting for global perception.
Language & Content
▶ ℹ Locale Assumptions 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG info · Locale Independence (Dates/Currency)
Element
Webflow currency settings script block
Detail
The Webflow currency settings are hardcoded to USD: '{"currencyCode":"USD","symbol":"$",...}' while the address points to New Zealand. While the site itself doesn't display transactional content requiring currency conversion, relying on USD default settings can be confusing for users expecting local NZD given the NZ address and TLD (.co.nz).
Fix
If this is a consultancy/services site and not e-commerce, remove or adjust the currency settings script if it causes ambiguity, or ensure that if any pricing elements appear later, they correctly reflect the local context (NZD) or use ISO codes.
▶ ℹ Exclusionary Phrasing 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG info · Ability Assumption Language
Element
Video caption: "Our foundation in human-centred digital product strategy and development provides a frictionless, inclusive experience for our clients and the people they serve."
Detail
The use of the word 'frictionless' in describing the experience implies an environment devoid of any difficulty or hindrance. While marketing language, this can inadvertently imply a lack of understanding for users who experience genuine friction due to disability, technical limitations, or systemic barriers.
Fix
Replace 'frictionless' with more concrete, active inclusive language, such as 'accessible, seamless, or intuitive experiences'.
Combined
▶ ℹ Geographic Bias 1 finding ℹ INFO
ℹ INFO WCAG info · Western/US-Centric Bias
Element
Footer link: Industries listing (e.g., Agriculture, Financial services, Health & wellbeing, Insurance services, Utilities)
Detail
The listed industries (Agriculture, Financial services, Health & wellbeing, Insurance services, Utilities) are general, but the inclusion of the 'Digital Nations Commitment' (in the Insights section, linking to /insights/digital-nations-commitment) strongly implies a focus on New Zealand/Commonwealth digital policy contexts, which might alienate or lack relevance for users outside that specific geographic/political framework.
Fix
If the company seeks international clients, consider framing policy mentions to focus on universal digital ethics rather than country-specific commitments, or ensure that the context provided in the linked content clarifies the geographic scope of such commitments.
